content.content:
# Tweet Text Extraction

The tweet text from the JSON response is:

"🚨 This Python tool just made vector databases optional for RAG. It's called PageIndex. It reads documents the way you do. No embeddings. No chunking. No vector database needed." 

The post continues to explain how PageIndex differs from traditional RAG systems by building tree structures similar to a table of contents, achieving 98.7% accuracy on FinanceBench, and supporting PDFs, markdown, and images. The tool is released as 100% open source under the MIT License.

input.prompt: Extract the tweet text from the JSON response

content.content:
# Tweet Text Extraction

The tweet discusses a GitHub repository called "production-agentic-rag-course" that teaches building RAG systems like industry companies do. It outlines a 7-week curriculum covering infrastructure, search fundamentals, and agentic systems.

Key quote: "if you want to build real ai systems, you have to understand the plumbing first."

The author emphasizes that successful approaches prioritize keyword search foundations before vector search, contrasting with tutorials that jump directly to AI-first methods.

# Tweet Text Extraction

The tweet text from the JSON response is:

"80% of AI beginners are confused by RAG Agents. Let's fix that (in under 2 minutes): a thread🧵"

This message was posted by Matt Dancho on March 19, 2026, and received significant engagement with over 19,800 views and 422 likes.

input.prompt: Extract the tweet text from the JSON response

content.content:
# Tweet Text Extraction

The tweet announces an open-source AI agency project called "The Agency" that gained 50K GitHub stars in under two weeks. Key features include:

**Core Offering:**
"147 specialized AI agents across 12 divisions -- engineering, design, marketing, product, QA, support, spatial computing"

**Main Capabilities:**
The project supports integration with Claude Code, GitHub Copilot, Gemini CLI, and other tools, featuring one-command installation, defined agent missions with success metrics, and modding support for custom agent creation.

**Key Insight:**
The tweet contrasts typical AI usage—"One model doing everything from writing copy to debugging code"—with this framework's approach of structuring AI as a specialized organization with defined roles and workflows.

**Project Stats:**
50K+ GitHub stars, 7.5K forks, released under MIT License, with contributions from global developers.

input.prompt: Extract the tweet text from the JSON response

content.content:
# Tweet Text Extraction

Here's the tweet text from the JSON response:

"i call BS on vector search for most use cases. everyone is building memory infrastructure. embeddings, vector stores, PageRank, spreading activation. co-occurrence learning. dampening pipelines. i gave @badlogicgames pi napkin, a CLI for obsidian vaults. BM25 search. TF-IDF based context overview. HotpotQA (250 questions, multi-hop): - 99.8% recall (vs 90% Ori, 29% Mem0) - 79.6% answer F1 (vs 41% Ori, 19% Mem0) haiku 4.5, markdown files on disk, no embeddings, no vector DB, no cloud. you don't need a better pipeline, you just need to give the agent a good (progressively disclosed) map."

The author is Michael Livs (@micLivs), posted on March 27, 2026, and received 431 likes and 38,616 views.

# Tweet Text Extraction

The tweet text from the JSON response is:

"16 best GitHub repos to build AI engineering projects! (star + bookmark them): The open-source AI ecosystem has 4.3M+ repos now. New repos blow up every month, and the tools developers build with today look nothing like what we had a year ago. I put together a visual covering the 16 repos that make up the modern AI developer toolkit right now..."

The full content continues through descriptions of 16 repositories spanning AI agent frameworks, model serving, fine-tuning tools, monitoring platforms, and data processing utilities, concluding with: "If you're building anything with LLMs, agents, or RAG in 2026, you're probably already using a few of these."

# Tweet Text Extraction

The tweet text from the JSON response is:

"Best GitHub repos for Claude code that will 10x your next project in 2026:

1. Claude Mem
2. UI UX Pro Max
3. n8n-MCP
4. Obsidian Skills
5. LightRAG
6. Everything Claude Code
7. Superpowers
8. Awesome Claude Code
9. GSD (Get Shit Done)"

The post, authored by Guri Singh (@heygurisingh), lists nine repositories recommended for Claude development projects, complete with direct GitHub links to each resource.

# Tweet Text Extraction

The main tweet from Sharbel states:

"Someone just open sourced a tool that lets your AI agents scrape Twitter, Reddit, YouTube, Github + more. Zero API fees."

The tweet goes on to describe practical applications, including integrating the tool with OpenClaw for real-time trend analysis, building market intelligence bots to identify pain points across social platforms, and automating research workflows.

The post quotes a GitHub Projects Community announcement highlighting a CLI tool that provides AI agents free access to read and search multiple platforms including Twitter, Reddit, YouTube, GitHub, Bilibili, and XiaoHongShu.

# Tweet Text Extraction

The main tweet discusses the fastest-growing GitHub repositories for the week. Here's the core content:

**Primary Focus:** The tweet highlights 10 trending GitHub projects, emphasizing that "the fastest growing GitHub repos this week" feature AI agent-related tools and frameworks.

**Key Projects Featured:**
The list includes repositories ranging from "+22.8K stars" for agent optimization tools to "+1.8K stars" for async coding agents. Notable entries mention "agent harness optimization," "agentic skills framework," and "open-source long-horizon SuperAgent" capabilities.

**Closing Statement:** The author concludes with "the theme this week: AI agents took over GitHub again" and advises readers to bookmark the list since "next week's list will look completely different."

The tweet also includes a quoted tweet from a week earlier with a similar format, showing trending projects from March 20, 2026.

The article discusses how static skills fail as environments change, advocating for self-improving skill systems through observation, inspection, amendment, and evaluation cycles using the cognee framework.
